perturbation.original_text: (b) This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of the State of New York applicable to agreements made and to be performed entirely within such State, without regard to the conflicts of law principles of such State. Each party shall comply in all respects with all laws and regulations applicable to its activities under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of the State of New York applicable to agreements made and to be performed entirely within such State, without regard to the conflicts of law principles of such State. Each party shall comply with all regulations applicable to its activities under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'laws' creates ambiguity regarding compliance with legal obligations, potentially contradicting consumer protection laws which are legally binding.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 349 prohibits deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in New York state. By omitting the explicit requirement to comply with 'all laws', the revised text creates a loophole that could be interpreted as permitting non-compliance with consumer protection laws, contradicting the statute's intent to protect consumers from deceptive business practices.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires compliance with 'all laws and regulations', while the changed text omits 'laws', only requiring compliance with 'all regulations'. This omission creates a loophole, as it could be argued that the contract only requires compliance with regulations and not with laws. According to the Law Explanation, NY GBL § 349 prohibits deceptive acts or practices. By omitting the explicit requirement to comply with 'all laws', the revised text introduces the possibility of non-compliance with consumer protection laws, contradicting the statute's intent. The scraped snippet indicates that Article 22-A of the General Business Law pertains to consumer protection from deceptive acts and practices, which are codified as laws. The omission therefore contradicts this consumer protection law.

perturbation.original_text: Late payments shall accrue interest at a rate equal to fifteen (15%) percent per annum.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the specific interest rate for late payments. This omission could violate usury laws if the 'may accrue interest' clause is later interpreted to allow for rates exceeding legal limits.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-501 sets the legal rate of interest in New York. While it doesn't mandate charging interest, removing the specified interest rate and replacing it with a vague 'may accrue interest' statement opens the door for potentially usurious rates to be applied later, especially if not explicitly agreed upon or disclosed, contradicting the spirit and intent of usury laws by failing to clearly define interest terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a fixed interest rate for late payments (15%). The changed text removes this specific rate, stating only that late payments 'may accrue interest.' This omission creates a contradiction because without a defined rate, the contract opens itself to potentially violating usury laws, as stated in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unavailable, the Law Explanation indicates that NY GOL § 5-501 is relevant. The omission of a specific interest rate, according to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, introduces ambiguity and the potential for rates exceeding legal limits, establishing a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Diplomat may, at its own expense, examine and copy those books and records as provided in this Section 4. Such books, records and accounts will be maintained in a manner that allows Diplomat to separate these matters from those relating to Tadeo's other operations. Such books, records and accounts will reflect such information as would normally be examined by an independent accountant in performing an audit pursuant to United States generally accepted auditing standards for the purpose of certifying financial statements, and to permit verification thereof by governmental agencies.
perturbation.changed_text: Diplomat may examine those books and records as provided in this Section 4. Such books, records and accounts will be maintained.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'at its own expense, and copy' and the specific requirements about maintaining records (auditing standards) hinders Diplomat's ability to verify Tadeo's accounting practices. This can create legal risk if Tadeo's financial reporting is inaccurate and Diplomat cannot verify it.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the contract predates Sarbanes-Oxley, the principle of internal controls over financial reporting is relevant. The original clause allows Diplomat to audit and verify financial information related to the services provided. By removing the ability to 'copy' and weakening the record-keeping requirements, Diplomat loses a critical mechanism for ensuring financial transparency, potentially creating legal risk related to misreporting or fraudulent activities.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 98 / SUBCHAPTER IV / § 7262 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 7262 : Management assessment of internal controls Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 98-PUBLIC COMPANY ACCOUNTING REFORM AND CORPORATE RESPONSIBILITY SUBCHAPTER IV-ENHANCED FINANCIAL DISCLOSURES Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §7262. Management assessment of internal controls (a) Rules required The Commission shall prescribe rules requiring each annual report required by section 78m(a) or 78o(d) of this title to contain an internal control report, which shall- (1) state the responsibility of management for establishing and maintaining an adequate internal control structure and procedures for financial reporting; and (2) contain an assessment, as of the end of the most recent fiscal year of the issuer, of the effectiveness of the internal control structure and procedures of the issuer for financial reporting. (b) Internal control evaluation and reporting With respect to the internal control assessment required by subsection (a), each registered public accounting firm that prepares or issues the audit report for the issuer, other than an issuer that is an emerging growth company (as defined in section 78c of this title ), shall attest to, and report on, the assessment made by the management of the issuer. An attestation made under this subsection shall be made in accordance with standards for attestation engagements issued or adopted by the Board. Any such attestation shall not be the subject of a separate engagement. (c) Exemption for smaller issuers Subsection (b) shall not apply with respect to any
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes Diplomat's ability to copy records and weakens the standards for maintaining those records. 15 USC 7262(a) requires companies to maintain an adequate internal control structure and procedures for financial reporting. The original contract language provided a mechanism for Diplomat to verify Tadeo's compliance with these standards, but the changed text removes this mechanism, creating a potential contradiction of the internal controls requirement.
